title: Bargaining with Voluntary Disclosure and Endogenous Matching
abstract: We investigate a bargaining setting between an “informed” player, who has private information, and an “uninformed” player. The informed player has the option to truthfully disclose private information in two unique environments. In the first environment, the informed player is randomly matched with an uninformed player and,aftermatching, can voluntarily disclose private information prior to a negotiation taking place. In the second environment, the informed player can voluntarily disclose private informationbeforeany endogenous matching between players (and subsequent negotiation) takes place. We begin by examining these environments theoretically. When disclosure occurs after matching, we show that low informed types should disclose more often than high informed types to avoid disagreement. However, when disclosure occurs before matching, for a range of parameter values, we show that high informed types should disclose more than low informed types to secure a better match. We test these predictions in a controlled human-subjects experiment and verify many of the theoretical predictions. Among other results, we find that low informed types do indeed disclose their private information to avoid disagreement and that, when it is in their interest to do so, high informed types disclose their private information to secure a better match. Another key insight is that high uninformed types benefit from disclosure regardless of whether it occurs before or after matching.

author.Andrew M. Davis: Samuel Curtis Johnson Graduate School of Management, SC Johnson College of Business, Cornell University, Ithaca, New York 14853
author.Kyle Hyndman: Naveen Jindal School of Management, University of Texas at Dallas, Richardson, Texas 75080

article.1. Introduction: Industry is replete with examples of companies wanting transparency from partner firms. A well-known example is Toyota. As a buyer, its purchasing department encourages suppliers to share their costs so that they can attempt to identify joint gains (Mishina 1995). Yet some suppliers may be reluctant to share their private information if they feel it will put them at a disadvantage in any subsequent negotiation. On the other hand, other suppliers may be willing to voluntarily disclose their private information if they believe it will improve their bargaining position or increase their chance of securing an agreement. Companies like Toyota encourage disclosure not just with suppliers with which they already have a relationship but also as a way of selecting new suppliers. This creates a second motivation for companies to disclose their private information: even if disclosure is voluntary, a reluctance to disclose may affect the likelihood of being invited to negotiate a contract with a prospective partner company. That is, companies may endogenously decide with whom they want to bargain with and base this decision partly on disclosure of private information. There is empirical evidence suggesting that these reasons for disclosure are consistent with practice. A study by McKinsey & Company shows that buyers who closely collaborated with their suppliers achieved earnings (before interest and tax) growth rates that were double compared with those that did not (Noor et al. 2012). This same study mentions that collaborative efforts included “negotiations [which] are based on full transparency into costs.” Our conversations with managers also provide further anecdotal evidence. For instance, one manager of a contract manufacturer indicated to us that their suppliers are expected to disclose the cause of any price changes and that buyers expect the same from them, even if the cause is because of an internal cost increase. Neglecting to disclose such information could put them at a competitive disadvantage. In addition, another product manager at a manufacturing company said that they are “constantly looking for suppliers who have an open book.” With this motivation, we investigate bargaining between two parties where one has private information. We extend this baseline paradigm in two important directions and answer the following research questions. First,afterbeing randomly matched together, if the two parties are about to engage in a negotiation, should the informed party truthfully disclose their private information? Second, if a party can disclose their private informationbeforebeing endogenously matched with another party and engaging in any subsequent negotiation, should that party truthfully disclose private information? A key feature of bargaining with private information is the possibility of disagreement, which is used as a (sometimes inefficient) tactic for extracting another party’s private information. Therefore, one key role of truthful information disclosure may be to eliminate the chance of disagreement by restoring complete information. However, when voluntary disclosure is available prior to any matching, there is an additional trade-off for the privately informed party: being matched with a (potentially) better partner at the cost of giving up information rents by disclosing versus being matched randomly with a (potentially) worse partner, but maintaining information rents in any negotiation. The overarching question we aim to examine is whether, and which types of, informed parties would find it optimal to disclose their private information. We address our two research questions both theoretically and experimentally in order to investigate the empirical relevance of disclosure both when matching isexogenousand disclosure occurs after matching, as well as when matching isendogenousand disclosure occurs before (and influences) matching. In both settings, we refer to the two different types of parties as “informed” players (e.g., suppliers with private cost information) and “uninformed” players (e.g., buyers). FollowingCamerer et al. (2019), we begin by building insights from mechanism design to generate theoretical predictions based on two focal-point equilibria: the equal-split equilibrium and the efficient equilibrium. In the former case, agreements prioritize equality of payoffs between the informed and uninformed players. As a result, incentive compatibility requires a strictly positive probability of disagreement for all informed player types except the highest.1Because the informed players do not receive any information rents in the equal-split equilibrium, this suggests that all but the highest informed player type should strictly prefer to disclose their private information in order to eliminate the chance of disagreement. On the other hand, in the efficient equilibrium, efficiency is prioritized over equality of payoffs. Therefore, disagreements are less common and only occur with relatively low informed player types (where disagreement is efficient). In this equilibrium, higher informed player types always reach agreement with the uninformed player and, additionally, earn information rents. Therefore, under the efficient equilibrium, disclosure should be concentrated on relatively low informed player types, whereas higher informed player types do not disclose and continue to reap their information rents. In contrast, when matching is a concern, the informed players need to consider the effect that disclosure would have on (i) the quality of the player they will be matched with, and (ii) the information rents that they will receive. Our theoretical analysis suggests that there may be multiple possible disclosure equilibria whether subsequent bargaining is represented by the efficient equilibrium focal point or by the equal-split equilibrium focal point. That said, there are substantive differences between the two focal point equilibria. Under the efficient equilibrium, because high types would lose information rents following a disclosure and because low types would be matched with a worse bargaining partner following disclosure, it is possible that no disclosure occurs in equilibrium even if the direct cost of disclosure is zero. In contrast, under the equal-split equilibrium, there is always a (positive) disclosure cost low enough such thatsomedisclosure must occur in equilibrium. The reason is twofold. First, high player types do not receive information rents, so they are motivated to disclose in order to match with a better bargaining partner. Second, low player types may also be motivated to disclose. Even though they will be matched with a worse bargaining partner, disclosure will restore full information and, therefore, eliminate the possibility of disagreement. These normative theoretical benchmarks are useful for a number of reasons. They can provide details about potential bargaining outcomes, depending on paired players’ priorities. When compared with empirical results, they can also serve as a stepping stone for additional theoretical extensions. Further, in practice, human managers are integral to a variety of operational transactions and decisions. Some examples include precisely those that we aim to study, notably disclosure decisions and bargaining. Therefore, in addition to our theoretical analysis, we conduct two controlled human-subject experiments. Observing how humans actually make decisions, relative to how they should make decisions, can both inform theory and yield important managerial insights. For example, such experiments can help researchers identify if the equilibria are useful in organizing the data, whereas comparisons within and across treatments can help managers identify when disclosure is beneficial (or costly), depending on their type and depending on whether voluntary disclosure is available before or after being matched with a partner company. Our experiment consists of three parts and follows our theoretical analysis. Part 1 acts as a baseline where disclosure is not possible and players simply bargain under private information. The informed player can be one of two equally likely types: high or low. The surplus over which a bargaining pair negotiates is larger for the high type than the low type. With this baseline, we can see how well the two focal point equilibria explain bargaining behavior. Consistent withCamerer et al. (2019), we find support for both equilibria. As predicted by the efficient equilibrium, the informed high type earns strictly positive information rents. At the same time, we do see some disagreement, and, consistent with the equal-split equilibrium, disagreement is more likely for the informed low type. The other two parts of our experiment allow us to study the “disclosure to avoid disagreement” and the “disclosure for matching” motivations for disclosure. Specifically, in part 2,afterpairs of informed and uninformed players are randomly formed, and prior to bargaining, the informed player has the option to truthfully disclose one’s type to the uninformed player by paying a strictly positive disclosure cost. In this case, the only motivation to disclose is to avoid disagreement. Consequently, theory predicts that only informed low types should disclose and only when the disclosure cost is low enough. Our experiment largely supports this prediction: low types disclose significantly more frequently than high types, and the frequency of disclosure is decreasing in the disclosure cost. In addition, when the informed player discloses, disagreements are significantly less likely. Consequently, consistent with the theoretical predictions, disclosure benefits informed low player types (we also observe that it benefits the uninformed player). In part 3 of our experiment, we investigate the role of voluntary disclosure when disclosure takes placebeforebargaining pairs have been formed, and matching is conditioned on the information contained in any disclosure decisions. We consider markets consisting of two informed and two uninformed players. To give additional incentives for disclosure, one uninformed player is relatively more attractive than the other in that the surplus is larger when matched with the more attractive uninformed player. As in part 2, each informed player is equally likely to be a high or a low type. We parameterize the experiment such that the unique disclosure equilibrium is for the informed high type only to disclose. Consistent with this prediction, we find that informed high types do indeed disclose significantly more often than informed low types, thereby increasing their chance of matching with the more desirable uninformed player as well as the likelihood of reaching an agreement. Thus, for informed high player types, disclosure is beneficial. In this setting, we also show that disclosure significantly helps uninformed high player types. Not only do they match with a better partner but also they receive a larger share of the pie than when bargaining with private information. Although our experimental results provide fairly strong support for the theoretical predictions, one concern is that we consider a single parameterization of our experiment, and human participants may be following behavioral rules that just so happen to coincide with the predictions. To address this, we provide results from a different parameterization of our human-subject experiment. When the only motive for disclosure is to avoid disagreement (as in part 2), we continue to see support for the prediction that low types are significantly more likely to disclose. When disclosure also affects the match (as in part 3), under the parameterization in this additional experiment, theory no longer predicts a difference in disclosure rates between high and low informed types, and this is precisely what we observe in the data. In sum, these additional experiments provide evidence that players respond to their incentives to disclose, rather than simply following behavioral rules.
article.2. Related Literature: The streams of literature most related to our study include papers that examine bargaining with private information, disclosure of information, and endogenous matching. Here, we provide a short summary of each of these three areas, paying particular attention to those works within economics, though we also briefly discuss some relevant papers from behavioral operations management. There is a large theoretical and experimental economics literature on bargaining with private information (seeRoth 1995,Muthoo 1999,Camerer 2003,Karagözoǧlu and Hyndman 2022for surveys of this literature). The two most relevant papers areCamerer et al. (2019)andForsythe et al. (1991). Like us, both of these papers apply insights from mechanism design to understand the role of private information, especially the likelihood of disagreement, and the explanatory power of different bargaining norms such as efficiency and equity. We build on their work by introducing the option for informed players to disclose their private information and examining how the timing of disclosure affects who discloses, if anyone, and how this affects subsequent behavior. Information disclosure has also been studied extensively within economics, as illustrated by two recent surveys (Dranove and Jin 2010,Loewenstein et al. 2014). Under voluntary disclosure, theory typically predicts full unraveling of (costless) disclosure, but there are exceptions. One prominent application concerns restaurant hygiene ratings from local health departments (e.g.,Bederson et al. 2018,Jin et al. 2021), where restaurants often have the option to voluntarily disclose their score—typically a letter grade. Theory predicts disclosure from the top down, where restaurants that receive the highest rating have a strict preference to disclose. This, in turn, forces those with the next highest rating to disclose (lest they be mistaken for having a lower rating), and so on. Whereas there is general support for the theory,Bederson et al. (2018)document an exception, showing a nonmonotonic disclosure pattern because of a “countersignaling” effect of nondisclosure by the highest types. Especially relevant to us areBoard (2009)andHotz and Xiao (2013), who show that disclosure need not lead to complete unraveling when there are strategic effects. We add to this literature by showing that disclosure predictions are dependent on the underlying bargaining norm. In particular, whether informed types earn information rents absent disclosure and how disclosure affects matching are key strategic factors that determine which informed types disclose. Our paper also relates to the literature on endogenous matching. Most closely related to our work areElliott and Nava (2019),Agranov and Elliott (2021), andEchenique et al. (2024), who study bargaining in matching markets, though with complete information and no information disclosure. These papers are generally interested—theoretically and/or experimentally—in the existence of efficient equilibria.Nalbantian and Schotter (1995)are an exception of studying matching markets with private information as we do. In contrast to our work, none of these papers study how disclosure affects matching. Finally, we would be remiss if we neglected to recognize the relevant literature within operations management. Recent experimental work has examined how two companies negotiate supply chain contract terms. Most of these papers assume full information and ultimatum offers from one party. Only more recent work has considered alternative forms of bargaining (Leider and Lovejoy 2016,Davis and Hyndman 2018,Davis and Leider 2018,Haruvy et al. 2020). Like some of these papers, we adopt an “unstructured” bargaining protocol, where two players are permitted to make unlimited back-and-forth offers over a fixed time frame. Within this area,Davis and Hyndman (2021)are especially noteworthy. They consider private information with unstructured bargaining, but their experiment is framed in a very specific context, whereas ours is more abstract, allowing for more general insights. Moreover, a key aspect of their study is that two companies are always randomly matched together. We extend this by allowing two parties to endogenously match after any disclosure decision, which yields important and novel insights. For example, we find that information disclosure can benefit high types and hurt low types. There are also papers in behavioral operations that allow for endogenous matching. Notable examples areFan et al. (2018),Beer et al. (2020), andHyndman and Honhon (2020); however, these papers do not generally focus on either disclosure or a rich bargaining environment like we do.
article.3. Theoretical Background: We seek to understand how the possibility of truthful information disclosure affects bargaining between players when, absent disclosure, there is private information. Additionally, we are interested in studying how disclosure may interact when the two players are either randomly matched together or when the matching between parties is endogenous and depends on disclosure decisions. The underlying foundation on which this theory is built is based onCamerer et al. (2019), who use insights from mechanism design to make predictions about behavior on bargaining with private information. In the following sections, we will first provide an overview and, like them, report predictions for two focal points: the efficient equilibrium and the equal-split equilibrium. We will then examine two additional settings with disclosure: one where disclosure is permitted after random matching and another where disclosure is permitted before endogenous matching. The interested reader can find further details in our Online Appendix (which focuses on the setting where matching is influenced by disclosure). In all cases, we consider an unstructured bargaining environment. That is, the two players can make as many offers as they like over a fixed time frame (until an agreement is reached or until time runs out and there is disagreement) with no requirement of alternating offers. 3.1. Setting 1: Bargaining with Private Information and No Disclosure We assume bargaining between two players, one of which is informed about the size of the pie and the other of which is uninformed but knows the distribution. To keep things simple and because it will coincide with the first part of our experiment, we assume that there are two possible pie sizes,πhandπl, with𝜋ℎ>𝜋𝑙πh>πland each pie being equally likely. In the experiment, we further assume that𝜋ℎ<2⁢𝜋𝑙πh<2⁢πl. This ensures that disagreements are necessarily inefficient.2 As a first step, we want to impose certain constraints on the possible bargaining outcomes. Specifically, we can imagine that the bargaining outcome is determined by the outcome of some mechanism. Then, drawing on the revelation principle, we can focus on direct revelation mechanisms and determine what restrictions incentive compatibility and individual rationality constraints impose. To this end, letwkdenote the payoff, conditional on reaching an agreement, to the uninformed player when the pie isπk. Additionally, letγkdenote the probability that an agreement is reached. Combining these two, the expected payoff to the uninformed player is denoted by𝑥𝑘≔𝛾𝑘⁢𝑤𝑘xk≔γk⁢wk, whereas the expected payoff to the informed player is𝛾𝑘⁢𝜋𝑘−𝑥𝑘γk⁢πk−xk. In order for the players to participate, individual rationality must be satisfied for both the informed and uninformed players. That is,𝑥𝑘≥0xk≥0and𝛾𝑘⁢𝜋𝑘−𝑥𝑘≥0γk⁢πk−xk≥0for𝑘∈{ℎ,𝑙}k∈{h,l}. Furthermore, incentive compatibility should also be satisfied for informed players. That is,𝛾ℎ⁢𝜋ℎ−𝑥ℎ≥𝛾𝑙⁢𝜋ℎ−𝑥𝑙γh⁢πh−xh≥γl⁢πh−xland𝛾𝑙⁢𝜋𝑙−𝑥𝑙≥𝛾ℎ⁢𝜋𝑙−𝑥ℎγl⁢πl−xl≥γh⁢πl−xh. Camerer et al. (2019)prove several properties about the bargaining outcome in any direct mechanism that is individually rational (IR) and incentive compatible (IC). We restate their result (Lemma 1) for our specific environment where there are only two types: The probability of agreement is increasing in pie size. That is,𝛾ℎ≥𝛾𝑙γh≥γl. The uninformed player’s expected payoffs are increasing in the pie size. That is,𝑥ℎ≥𝑥𝑙xh≥xl. If𝛾ℎ=𝛾𝑙=1γh=γl=1, thenxh=xl. That is, the uninformed player’s expected payoff is independent of the true pie size if disagreement is never predicted to occur. Although these results are proved in greater generality elsewhere (Forsythe et al. 1991,Camerer et al. 2019), it is instructive to illustrate the arguments here for completeness. To see the first part, note that incentive compatibility implies that Rearranging yields Because𝜋ℎ−𝜋𝑙>0πh−πl>0by assumption, this gives us𝛾ℎ≥𝛾𝑙γh≥γl. Now that we know𝛾ℎ≥𝛾𝑙γh≥γl, the second part of the lemma easily follows from (2); that is,𝑥ℎ≥𝑥𝑙xh≥xl. Finally, for the third part, under the assumption that𝛾ℎ=𝛾𝑙=1γh=γl=1, then (2) reduces toxh=xl. Return now to the question of whether efficiency is compatible with disagreement and our assumption that𝜋ℎ<2⁢𝜋𝑙πh<2⁢πl. First, observe that disagreement is never efficient in the high state; that is,𝛾ℎ=1γh=1.3Second, efficiency requires that the incentive compatibility constraint holds with equality in the high state; that is,𝑥ℎ−𝑥𝑙=(1−𝛾𝑙)⁢𝜋ℎxh−xl=(1−γl)⁢πh.4To complete the analysis, consider a mechanism𝜇=(𝛾ℎ=1,𝛾𝑙<1,𝑥ℎ,𝑥𝑙)μ=(γh=1,γl<1,xh,xl)and suppose that it is not efficient. This implies that𝛿>0δ>0. It also implies that𝛿⁢𝜋𝑙≥𝑑≥𝛿⁢𝜋ℎ/2δ⁢πl≥d≥δ⁢πh/2, which gives us𝜋ℎ<2⁢𝜋𝑙πh<2⁢πl. That is, if this condition is satisfied (which we will assume in our experiments), then disagreements are always inefficient. Now that we have outlined the constraints imposed on bargaining by incentive compatibility and individual rationality, we turn our attention to two specific notions of equilibrium that satisfy these constraints. The two focal points each prioritize two opposing but normatively desirable criteria: efficiency and equity. 3.1.1. Focal Point 1: Efficiency. Given our assumption that𝜋ℎ<2⁢𝜋𝑙πh<2⁢πl, in an efficient equilibrium, disagreements should never arise. That is,𝛾ℎ=𝛾𝑙=1γh=γl=1. This, in turn, implies that the uninformed player should receive the same payoff regardless of the pie. That is,xh=xl. Without further assumptions, any𝑥𝑙∈[0,𝜋𝑙]xl∈[0,πl]would satisfy the IC and IR constraints. However, it is at this point where we call upon equity as a secondary criterion. That is, we assume that𝑥𝑙=𝜋𝑙/2xl=πl/2. Therefore, when the pie is low, the players share the pie equally, whereas when the pie is high, the informed player reaps all the information rents. 3.1.2. Focal Point 2: Equal Split. In this equilibrium, equality of payoffs is prioritized at the expense of efficiency. In particular, we require that𝑤𝑒⁢𝑞𝑘=𝜋𝑘/2wke⁢q=πk/2for𝑘∈{ℎ,𝑙}k∈{h,l}. This then implies that efficiency can only be obtained forπh; that is,𝛾𝑒⁢𝑞ℎ=1γhe⁢q=1. Plugging in𝑤𝑒⁢𝑞𝑘wke⁢qand𝛾ℎ=1γh=1into the IC constraints leads to 3.2. Setting 2: Bargaining with Private Information and Disclosure After Matching With the two focal point equilibria as given, we now consider the situation in which, prior to bargaining (but after learning their type), informed players can make a credible disclosure about their type—at cost𝑑≥0d≥0. If the informed player discloses, then the size of the pie is common knowledge and our operating assumption (based on extensive experimental literature as well as predictions from the Nash bargaining solution with equal bargaining power (Nash 1950,Nydegger and Owen 1974)) is that the players will split the pie equally and there will be no disagreement. That is,𝛾𝑑𝑘=1γkd=1and𝑤𝑑𝑘=𝜋𝑘/2wkd=πk/2, where the superscriptddenotes that a disclosure has occurred. For each of the focal point equilibria given, we seek to determine whether any types disclose and, if so, whether disclosure by one type leads to unraveling whereby all types are forced to disclose. When bargaining pairs are randomly matched, irrespective of their type and any disclosure decision, the primary motive for an informed player to disclose is to avoid disagreement. Consider a typekinformed player and letγkandwkdenote the agreement probability and payoff to the uninformed player, respectively, absent disclosure. This player will disclose if 3.2.1. Efficient Equilibrium. Under this mechanism, given our assumption that disagreementsneveroccur and given that informed players always earn weakly more than𝜋𝑘/2πk/2for𝑘∈{ℎ,𝑙}k∈{h,l}, (5) can never be satisfied. That is, in our experiments, under the efficient equilibrium, no informed type should ever disclose for a strictly positive costd> 0. Ifd= 0, then the low type is indifferent between disclosing and not disclosing, whereas the high type still strictly prefers to not disclose. 3.2.2. Equal-Split Equilibrium. In this case,𝑤𝑘=𝜋𝑘/2wk=πk/2for all𝑘∈{ℎ,𝑙}k∈{h,l}and𝛾𝑙<1γl<1. In this case, (5) will be satisfied for the low type (i.e.,k=l), provided thatdis sufficiently small. That is, the low type is willing to disclose, for low enough cost, in order to avoid the chance of disagreement.5 Thus, we have: In the efficient equilibrium,(i)for all𝑑≥0d≥0,the informed high type (h) never discloses, and(ii)the informed low type (l) never discloses for any d >0and is indifferent for d =0. In the equal-split equilibrium,(i)there exists a̅𝑑>0d¯>0such that for𝑑<̅𝑑d<d¯,the informed low type (l) prefers to disclose, and(ii)the informed high type (h) never discloses for any d >0and is indifferent for d =0. 3.3. Setting 3: Bargaining with Private Information and Disclosure Before Matching We now wish to consider another possible motivation for disclosure: namely, there may be heterogeneity in match quality, and the desire to match with a higher-quality partner may drive some informed players to disclose. Each uninformed player would certainly prefer to match with the high informed type than the informed low type (if types were known). We will assume that the same holds for informed players: that is, one uninformed player is a more desirable partner than the other. In this case, informed players may face a trade-off between being matched randomly (and so, with a potentially worse partner) but maintaining information rents or being matched with a better partner at the cost of giving up information rents by disclosing. Consider a market with four players, two of which are informed (indexed byi1andi2) and two of which are uninformed (indexed byuhandul). We assume that each informed player is equally likely to be a high type (h) or a low type (l). This ensures that disclosure by one informed player does not automatically reveal the other informed player’s type. To be sure, players will form beliefs about the type of any nondisclosing player, and these beliefs may depend on the equilibrium under consideration. On the other hand, to keep our analysis simple, we assume that one uninformed player (uh) is known to be a high type and the other is known to be a low type (ul). The surplus over which players bargain depends on how the players are matched, as inTable 2. Table 2.Bargaining Surplus Given Matching Table 2.Bargaining Surplus Given Matching A matching involvingulgenerates surplusπhorπl, depending on whether the informed player is a high or low type, whereas a matching involvinguhgenerates surplus𝛼ℎ⁢𝜋ℎαh⁢πhor𝛼ℎ⁢𝜋𝑙αh⁢πl, depending on whether the informed player is a high or low type. To capture thatuhis the more desirable bargaining partner, we assume that𝛼ℎ>1αh>1. The informed player knows the true bargaining surplus, whereas the uninformed player only knows the distribution. We maintain our previous assumption that𝜋ℎ<2⁢𝜋𝑙πh<2⁢πlto ensure that disagreement is necessarily inefficient. We assume that, after learning their types, the informed players simultaneously decide whether to disclose at cost𝑑≥0d≥0. The uninformed players observe the disclosure decision and then matching occurs, where we assume that the uninformed high type (uh) has priority.6After matching has occurred, negotiation takes place separately for each of the bargaining pairs. There are four possible pure disclosure equilibria: (i) neither informed type discloses, (ii) only the informed high type discloses, (iii) only the informed low type discloses, and (iv) both informed types disclose. It turns out that for both the efficient and equal-split equilibria, there exist parameter values such that each of the disclosure equilibria exists. It is also possible that, for a given set of parameter values(𝛼ℎ,𝜋ℎ,𝜋𝑙)(αh,πh,πl), multiple disclosure equilibria simultaneously exist. This is because the uninformed players’ beliefs are very different for the different equilibria, which may change an informed player’s motivation in favor of or against disclosing. For example, in the efficient equilibrium, under no disclosure, the informed low type does not disclose because disclosure does not improve match quality, nor does it favorably change the agreement rate but is, itself, a costly action. On the other hand, in the equilibrium where only the low type discloses, the low type is, in a sense, pressured to disclose because, otherwise, the low type will be mistaken for a high type and forced to give up a larger share of the surplus. As long as the cost of disclosure is not too high, the low type will disclose to prevent the mistaken identity. As we showed in the previous subsection, when disclosure is only a tool to eliminate the possibility of disagreement, informed high types never disclose for any positive cost. Our purpose here is to demonstrate that when disclosure can also be used as a tool to influence matching, high types may have an incentive to disclose. To this end, we now illustrate the conditions for the equilibrium in which the informed high type only discloses. Because this is a separating equilibrium, the beliefs of the uninformed players are always degenerate, both on and off the equilibrium path. Therefore, the analysis that follows applies to both the efficient and equal-split equilibrium. Consider an informed high type. By disclosing, the informed high type ensures a 75% chance of matching with the uninformed high type.7The payoff from disclosing is then On the other hand, by not disclosing, the informed high type will be assumed to be the low type and will be matched with the uninformed low type unless the other informed player also does not disclose. In this case, the informed high type’s payoffs are For disclosure to be optimal, it must be that the former is greater than the latter. That is, This is likely to be satisfied whenαhis relatively large, meaning that the uninformed high type is especially attractive, and when the difference between the informed high and informed low types is relatively small. To complete the demonstration, we should also show that the informed low type prefers nondisclosure. However, because the informed low type is already assumed to be the low type regardless of disclosing or not, the informed low type would rather save the disclosure cost and not disclose. It is possible to go further. Specifically, There exists a nondegenerate set of parameter values such that, for both the efficient and the equal-split bargaining equilibrium, the unique disclosure equilibrium is the one in which only the informed high type discloses. For a specific pair(𝜋𝑙,𝜋ℎ)(πl,πh),Figure 1delineates the regions for which each possible disclosure equilibrium holds. ComparingFigure 1(a)andFigure 1(b), we see that the for the efficient equilibrium, the region of uniqueness given inResult 2is relatively small (the shaded gray area below the blue solid line and above the dotted green line). In contrast, under the equal-split equilibrium, the region of uniqueness is rather large (the shaded gray area below the dashed-dotted red line and above the dotted green line). The reason for this difference is because disclosure is relatively more advantageous in the equal-split equilibrium than in the efficient equilibrium. Whereas in both cases, the informed high type incurs a direct disclosure cost and receives as benefit an increased likelihood of matching with the uninformed high type, in the efficient equilibrium, the informed high type must also sacrifice the information rents earned because of private information. This makes it so that “no disclosure” is an equilibrium for a relatively large set of parameters. In contrast, under the equal-split equilibrium, the informed high type does not lose information rents when disclosing—the informed high type receives half the pie regardless. This makes disclosure more attractive relative to nondisclosure. Observe that in the equal-split equilibrium (Figure 1(b)), there is a region where a pure strategy disclosure equilibrium does not exist. This corresponds to the region where disclosure costs are not sufficiently high to completely eliminate disclosure but too high to support an equilibrium where the high type only discloses for sure. In this region, no disclosure is not an equilibrium because the high type can deviate and disclose and, consequently, guarantee to be matched with the uninformed high type. High-only disclosing is not an equilibrium because, if it were, then disclosure by the high type only ensures a 75% chance of being matched with the uninformed high type. With only a 75% chance of receiving a favorable match, the disclosure cost must be significantly lower to induce disclosure. Whereas we note this possible nonexistence of pure disclosure equilibria, we do not pursue it further. This is because our goal is to show a motivation for the informed high type to disclose. AsResult 2shows, we are able to do this by focusing on pure disclosure equilibria.
article.4. Experimental Design: We now outline our design for our experiment, which allows us to address the issue of disclosure in bargaining both when the only purpose of disclosure is to reveal private information and when disclosure may also influence the matching of informed and uninformed players. The experiment consisted of three parts, conducted within subject, which directly parallel the three settings outlined inSections 3.1,3.2, and3.3. In all three parts, half of the players were informed players, and half were uninformed players. Subjects kept their role for the duration of the experiment. In addition, in each round, both informed and uninformed players were equally likely to be either a typeHor a typeLplayer (referred to as types 1 and 2, respectively, in the experiment). The pie sizes when an informed and uninformed player of each type are matched in a bargaining pair are given inTable 3. Informed players knew the true size of the pie. Uninformed players knew their own type but not the informed player’s type. Hence, they knew that the pie was equally likely to be one of the two elements in each row (corresponding to their type, that is, $45 or $36 for type H or $20 or $16 for type L). Table 3.Pie Sizes for Each Possible Matching in the Experiments Table 3.Pie Sizes for Each Possible Matching in the Experiments Notes. In the notation of the theory, these parameters imply that𝜋ℎ=20,𝜋𝑙=16πh=20,πl=16, and𝛼ℎ=2.25αh=2.25. All values are in $. The three parts of the experiment differ according to the presence and timing of disclosure. The design elements that are unique to each part are summarized inTable 4. Before providing further information around the unique details, we summarize the common components. In all three parts, each round concludes with a bargaining phase between one informed and one uninformed player. In each round, bargaining pairs had 60 seconds to negotiate over the payoff to theuninformed player. Players exchanged offers in an unstructured bargaining environment where they could make as many offers as they liked with no requirement of alternating offers, and if an agreement was reached before bargaining expired, the uninformed player would receive the agreed-upon amount, whereas the informed player would receive the difference between the true size of the pie and the agreed amount. If time expired without an agreement, each player received a payoff of zero. The feedback at the end of each round revealed each player’s payoffs, but importantly, the uninformed player would only learn the true pie size (and therefore the informed player’s actual payoff) if the informed player disclosed; otherwise, the uninformed player learned thepossiblepayoffs of the informed player. Table 4.Unique Design Details by Each Part of the Experiment Table 4.Unique Design Details by Each Part of the Experiment Notes. Part 1 corresponds with no disclosure (Section 3.1). Part 2 corresponds with disclosure after matching (Section 3.2). Part 3 corresponds with disclosure before matching (Section 3.3). All experiments were conducted at Cornell University in the spring of 2023. The experiments were conducted in person and took 75 minutes on average. In total, 92 subjects participated across six sessions, each with either 12 or 16 subjects. In addition to a show-up fee of $7, participants were paid for one randomly selected round from each of the three parts of the experiment. Average total earnings were roughly $46, with a minimum of $12 and maximum of $87, thus ensuring sufficient incentives. 4.1. Unique Design Details for Part 1: Private Information and No Disclosure Consistent withSection 3.1, there were no unique details in part 1 beyond those given earlier. That is, there was no disclosure decision and pairs bargained for up to 60 seconds. This part consisted of six rounds with random rematching of pairs between an uninformed and informed player in each round.Table 5contains the predicted negotiated agreements for each of the possible bargaining combinations and for both equilibrium norms. Table 5.Predictions For Bargaining with Private Information (and Disclosure in Part 2) Table 5.Predictions For Bargaining with Private Information (and Disclosure in Part 2) 4.2. Unique Design Details for Part 2: Private Information and Disclosure After Matching At the end of part 1, subjects were given instructions that informed them of the design changes and are summarized inTable 4. In part 2, after being matched (and learning their matched uninformed player’s type), the informed player now had the option to pay a disclosure cost and reveal their type to their match. If the informed player chose to disclose, then the uninformed player learned the informed player’s type and, hence, the true size of the pie. If the informed player chose not to disclose, then the choice to not disclose was communicated to the uninformed player, but no information about the informed player’s type was revealed. After the disclosure decision was made, the bargaining pair then had 60 seconds to negotiate an agreement, as in part 1. Part 2 allows us to test the “disclosure to avoid disagreement” motivation for disclosure, coinciding with the setting we outlined inSection 3.2. Under the equal-split bargaining norm, disagreement occurs with positive probability for relatively lower informed types (cf.Table 5). If the cost of disclosure is low enough, the informed player may find disclosure optimal to eliminate the possibility of disagreement by restoring complete information about the bargaining pie. For our specific experimental design, disclosure should only occur under the equal-split equilibrium, and only typeLinformed players should disclose when the cost is low enough.8This is displayed inTable 5in the columns “Disclose (part 2).” 4.3. Unique Design Details for Part 3: Private Information and Disclosure Before Matching At the end of part 2, additional instructions were provided to participants to explain the disclosure and matching process for part 3. As noted inTable 4, at the beginning of each period, groups of four participants were randomly formed, consisting of two informed types, each of whose type is randomly and independently drawn, one typeHuninformed player and one typeLuninformed player. Consistent with the theory outlined inSection 3.3, this ensures that disclosure by one informed player does not automatically reveal the other informed player’s type. It also meant that in each larger group of four, there was always a more and less desirable bargaining partner for the informed players. After groups of four were formed and players learned their types, each informed player would simultaneously decide whether to disclose that player’s type. They made this decision knowing the following rules for matching: Ifneitherinformed player chooses to disclose, then matching is done at random, with each pair consisting of one informed player and one uninformed player. If atypeHinformed player discloses one’s type, then this player will be automatically matched with the typeHuninformed playerunless the other informed player disclosed and is also a type H player. If that is the case, then matching is done at random. If atypeLinformed player discloses one’s type, then this player will be automatically matched with the typeLuninformed playerunless the other informed player disclosed and is also a type L player. If that is the case, then matching is done at random. As in part 2, disclosure was costly, with the cost either $0.75, $1.75, or $2.75 in any given round. As in part 2, the uninformed players were also informed of the disclosure cost faced by the informed players. Following the disclosure decision by both informed players, bargaining pairs, consisting of one informed and one uninformed player, were formed in accordance with the matching rules described. As in the previous parts, bargaining pairs had 60 seconds to negotiate an agreement. Different from parts 1 and 2, part 3 consisted of eight rounds. We chose to have more rounds because, as the most intricate design, subjects may have needed time to familiarize themselves with the environment. Part 3 allows us to test the “disclosure for matching” motivation for disclosure, which coincides withSection 3.3. To contrast with part 2, where it is relatively low informed types who disclose to avoid disagreement, we chose parameter values such that only high informed types should disclose (regardless of whether bargaining is best described by the efficient or the equal-split equilibrium). There is one qualification that merits discussion. Our baseline parameter set includes a disclosure cost of $2.75. Under both equilibrium norms, only the typeHinformed player is predicted to disclose (recallFigure 1). For the other two disclosure costs ($0.75 and $1.75), the prediction depends on the norm. For the equal-split equilibrium, the only disclosure equilibrium is for the typeHinformed player only to disclose. However, for the efficient equilibrium, there are two disclosure equilibria: typeHonly discloses, and both types disclose. We made the decision to use these parameters, despite the multiplicity in some cases, because we did not want to tie ourselves too strongly to the exact theory. For example, suppose informed players perceive the disclosure cost as a loss and overvalue it. In this case, even if every other aspect of the theory held true, the typeHinformed player would not disclose for a cost of $2.75. Yet in this case, we would still expect the typeHinformed player to be the only type to disclose; it is just that the dollar threshold is lower.
article.5. Experimental Results: In the following sections, we detail our experimental results, following the same structure as the previous two sections. 5.1. Part 1: Private Information and No Disclosure We begin the discussion of our experimental results by focusing on part 1, which represents a baseline setting where the informed player was unable to disclose and matching was done at random each period. InFigure 2, we summarize the payoffs conditional on an agreement of the informed player (Figure 2(a)), the uninformed player (Figure 2(b)), and the frequency of agreements (Figure 2(c)). The figures are broken up by the pie size that players actually bargained over. For example, “(𝐻,𝐿):45(H,L):45” indicates that the uninformed player was typeH, the informed player was typeL, and the pie size was $45. For each pie size, there are three bars: (i) the theoretical prediction for the efficient equilibrium, (ii) the observed “actual” data, and (iii) the theoretical prediction for the equal-split equilibrium. Beginning with informed players (Figure 2(a)), we observe that the two equilibrium concepts closely capture actual payoffs. First, the informed typeLplayer, regardless of which uninformed type they are matched with, receives approximately half the pie ($17.5 and $7.1 for pie sizes of $36 and $16). This is consistent with both the efficient and equal-split equilibria. Second, the informed typeHplayer, when matched with the uninformed typeHplayer (pie size of $45), earns substantially more than half the pie ($25.8), consistent with the efficient equilibrium. Finally, the informed typeHplayer, when matched with the uninformed typeLplayer (pie size of $20), earns $10.5 on average, which represents a small information rent but is somewhat closer to the equal-split equilibrium. Turning toFigure 2(b), looking at the bars corresponding to an uninformed typeHplayer, the efficient equilibrium provides a good description of behavior. As predicted by this equilibrium, the uninformed player’s payoff is approximately identical ($19.2 and $18.5) when the pie size is $45 and $36. Furthermore, inFigure 2(c), the frequencies of agreements are broadly similar for these two pie sizes as well, 91.1% and 86.4%.9Returning toFigure 2(b), when the uninformed player is typeL, we see a hybrid of the equal-split and efficient equilibria. Consistent with the efficient equilibrium is that the uninformed player’s payoff is relatively flat ($9.5 and $8.9) when the pie size is $20 and $16, although both amounts are slightly higher than the point predictions of $8. Consistent with the equal-split equilibrium, inFigure 2(c), we see that the frequency of agreements for a pie size of $16 is 83.1%, which is virtually identical to the prediction of 83.3%. Furthermore, as predicted, agreements are substantially more likely (97.0%) when the pie size is $20.10 (No Disclosure).Consistent withCamerer et al.(2019), behavior is well described by a combination of the efficient and equal-split bargaining norms. Given that behavior in this simple framework closely matches the two equilibrium concepts that we discussed, we are hopeful that theory will continue to serve as a useful guide when we introduce disclosure in the following sections. 5.2. Part 2: Private Information and Disclosure After Matching Recall that in part 2, after learning their own type and the type of their matched bargaining partner, informed players had the option to disclose their type. If they so chose, then the true size of the pie would become common knowledge by both parties. If they did not disclose, then the uninformed player only knew the possible pie sizes ($45 or $36 if the uninformed player was typeHand $20 or $16 if the uninformed player was typeL). Regardless of the equilibrium concept, typeHinformed players should not disclose, whereas typeLinformed players may disclose under the equal-split equilibrium, provided that the disclosure cost is low enough. InFigure 3, we plot the disclosure frequencies by informed players for each possible pie size, broken down by the disclosure cost. The aforementioned predictions are generally borne out. TypeHinformed players rarely disclose (first and third sets of columns), whereas typeLinformed players are much more likely to disclose (second and fourth sets of columns). Furthermore, the likelihood of disclosure is higher (as it should be) when matched with a typeHuninformed player than a typeLuninformed player. Last, with the exception of the pie size of $45, the frequency of disclosure is decreasing in the cost of disclosure (black versus charcoal versus light-gray columns).11 We can now see how the likelihood of agreement as well as the bargaining outcomes are influenced by the disclosure decisions of informed players.Table 6contains this information. For all four possible bargaining pies, we see that agreements are substantially more likely following disclosure (“no” versus “yes” columns). Indeed, disagreements never occur for three of the four possible bargaining pies. It is interesting to find that disagreement following disclosure of the lowest pie (i.e., a bargaining pair consisting of typeLinformed and uninformed players in the first row of the table) happens nearly 15% of the time (agreement rate, 85.7%). We believe that this is because the informed players attempt to recoup some of the disclosure cost that they incurred. Indeed, conditional on agreement, informed players actually earn nearly $1 more following disclosure ($8.42 versus $7.52). Such an attempt to recoup the disclosure cost could increase bargaining tensions, as the uninformed players likely view the 50-50 split as fair, whereas the informed players want more than 50% of the pie as compensation for disclosing. These competing norms could explain the nonnegligible disagreement rate. We see a similar result for the (H,L) bargaining pair in the third row of the table, where the pie size is $36 and disclosure is predicted to occur. Informed players who disclosed in this case earn $1.44 more on average ($18.63 versus $17.19). In addition to lowering the possibility of disagreement, disclosure also leads to a higher negotiated agreement, thereby enhancing the benefits of disclosure. Finally, observe that consistent with theory, when typeHinformed players disclose, their negotiated outcomes are actually lower than when they do not disclose ($9.14 versus $10.62 and $23.85 versus $24.35). Table 6.Summary Statistics on Agreement Rates and Bargaining Outcomes Conditional on Agreement Table 6.Summary Statistics on Agreement Rates and Bargaining Outcomes Conditional on Agreement Notes. “(X,Y)” represents the types of players,𝑋,𝑌∈{𝐻,𝐿}X,Y∈{H,L}, where the first element represents the uninformed player’s type, whereas the second element represents the informed player’s type. “N.D.” indicates that the informed playerdid notdisclose, whereas “Disc.” indicates that the informed playerdiddisclose. We are now in a position to quantify which roles (informed and/or uninformed) and which types (HorL) benefit from informed players’ disclosing. To this end, inTable 7, we report the estimated marginal effects of final payoffs (inclusive of disclosure costs for informed players) unconditional on whether an agreement was reached. Consider first informed players. As can be seen in the second row, there is a negative and weakly significant effect of disclosure when a typeHinformed player, matched with a typeLuninformed player, discloses (−1.753). This is not surprising because theory predicts no role for disclosure for informed typeHplayers.12We see that informed typeLplayers, when matched with uninformed typeHplayers, in the third row, significantly benefit from disclosure (2.678). Not only does disclosure eliminate disagreement but, also, the informed players appear able to partially offset the disclosure cost that they incurred. Table 7.Estimated Marginal Effects of Benefits Because of Disclosure (Net of Disclosure Cost) Table 7.Estimated Marginal Effects of Benefits Because of Disclosure (Net of Disclosure Cost) Notes. “(X,Y)” represents the types of players,𝑋,𝑌∈{𝐻,𝐿}X,Y∈{H,L}, where the first element represents the uninformed player’s type, whereas the second element represents the informed player’s type. The underlying regression is a random-effects regression (one for the informed player and one for the uninformed player), where the dependent variable is the final payoff at the end of bargaining (inclusive of any disclosure cost for the informed player), and the independent variables are indicators for the type of each player and an indicator for disclosure, as well as a full set of interactions. The table reports the estimated marginal effects at the specific bargaining pair type combination. Standard errors in parentheses were corrected for clustering at the session level. *, **, and *** denote significance at the 10%, 5%, and 1% levels, respectively. We turn now to uninformed players. As can be seen in the last two columns, disclosure by the informed player never hurts, and it significantly helps when a typeHinformed player discloses (2.711 and 2.375). It is somewhat surprising that uninformed players do not benefit when a typeLinformed player discloses. In theory, this should (and mostly does) eliminate disagreement, which is beneficial. However, we also observed a countervailing factor in which these informed players appeared to bargain more aggressively in order to recover at least part of the disclosure cost. The net result of these two factors is a modestly positive (but not significant) effect. Before proceeding, one other interesting finding merits discussion.Table 8shows that informed players, at least low types, are able to recoup some of the costs from disclosure. For uninformed players, the coefficients on𝐼𝑗×𝐷𝑖𝑠𝑐.𝐶𝑜𝑠𝑡𝐼𝑛𝑐𝑢𝑟𝑟𝑒𝑑Ij×Disc.CostIncurredshould be zero (if equal split) or positive (if efficient). This is because disclosure by the informed player should either not affect (equal split) or increase (efficient) the payoff of the uninformed player conditional on agreement. In contrast, the coefficients should be at least −1 (or smaller) for informed types. This is because the informed player’s final payoff is the bargaining payoff minus any disclosure cost incurred. In the equal-split equilibrium, bargaining payoffs conditional on agreement should not change, whereas they should decrease for the high type under the efficient equilibrium. The fact that the coefficient on𝐼𝐿×𝐷𝑖𝑠𝑐.𝐶𝑜𝑠𝑡𝐼𝑛𝑐𝑢𝑟𝑟𝑒𝑑IL×Disc.CostIncurredis significantly negative for uninformed players and significantly greater than −1 for informed players suggests that informed low types are able to recover some of the costs from disclosure with a more favorable agreement. Table 8.Estimated Final Payoffs and Disclosure Cost Incurred By Informed Player (Conditional on Agreement) Table 8.Estimated Final Payoffs and Disclosure Cost Incurred By Informed Player (Conditional on Agreement) Notes.IHis an indicator for the informed typeHplayer, whereasILis an indicator for the informed typeLplayer.Disc. Cost Incurredis a variable indicating the actual disclosure cost paid by the informed player. If the informed player did not disclose, then the value is zero, whereas it is the disclosure cost for the period if the informed player did disclose. Standard errors in parentheses were corrected for clustering at the session level. *, **, and *** denote significance at the 10%, 5%, and 1% levels, respectively. We summarize our findings as follows: (Disclosure to Avoid Disagreement).Consistent with the theoretical predictions, low informed types disclose significantly more often than high informed types, and disagreements are rare following disclosure. Disclosure is also more frequent when matched with an uninformed high type and declines as the cost of disclosure increases. Behaviorally, we see that informed low types who disclose are able to recover some of the disclosure cost during bargaining. 5.3. Part 3: Private Information and Disclosure Before Matching Finally, we turn our attention to part 3, where informed players made their disclosure decision after learning their type, but before matching. The disclosure decisions of both informed players would then determine which of the uninformed players would disclose. As our theoretical analysis showed, for both equilibrium concepts and all disclosure costs, there is an equilibrium where only the typeHinformed player discloses.13Figure 4plots the average frequency of disclosure by type and by disclosure cost. As can be seen, for every disclosure cost, typeHinformed players are substantially more likely to disclose than their typeLcounterparts.14 Table 9provides the same information as in part 2, and the results are broadly consistent. To summarize, disclosure again leads to a greater chance of agreement conditional on the match (“no” versus “yes”). Moreover, most informed players who disclosed earned more (conditional on the match) than those who did not (last two columns); the only exception was when two typeHplayers met and the pie was $45. This provides further suggestive evidence that, at least sometimes, informed players are able to offset their disclosure costs. Table 9.Summary Statistics on Agreement Rates and Bargaining Outcomes Conditional on Agreement Table 9.Summary Statistics on Agreement Rates and Bargaining Outcomes Conditional on Agreement Notes. “(X,Y)” represents the types of players,𝑋,𝑌∈{𝐻,𝐿}X,Y∈{H,L}, where the first element represents the uninformed player’s type, whereas the second element represents the informed player’s type. “N.D.” indicates that the informed playerdid notdisclose, whereas “Disc.” indicates that the informed playerdiddisclose. It is important to recognize thatTable 9cannot say whether a particular player benefited from disclosure because that analysis is conditional on the matching, whereas matching itself depends on the disclosure decision. To gain insight into this, we look atTables 10and11. Starting withTable 10, we observe three distinct effects: (i) disclosure by a typeHplayer increases the likelihood of being matched with a typeHuninformed player, whereas disclosure by a typeLplayer reduces the likelihood (first set of columns); (ii) disclosure, regardless of type, increases the chance of agreement (second set of columns); and (iii) disclosure benefits informed typeHplayers and hurts informed typeLplayers (last two sets of columns). Table 10.The Impact of Disclosure on Matching, Agreements, and Payoffs for Informed Player Table 10.The Impact of Disclosure on Matching, Agreements, and Payoffs for Informed Player Notes. The dependent variable for each regression is in the column heading.UHdenotes the uninformed high type.IHis an indicator for the informed typeHplayer, whereasILis an indicator for the informed typeLplayer.Disclosedis an indicator variable that takes value one if the informed player disclosed and zero otherwise. Payoffs are inclusive of any disclosure cost incurred by the informed player. Standard errors in parentheses were corrected for clustering at the session level. *, **, and *** denote significance at the 10%, 5%, and 1% levels, respectively. Table 11.Estimated Marginal Effects of Disclosure by Informed Player (Pooled and by Disclosure Cost) Table 11.Estimated Marginal Effects of Disclosure by Informed Player (Pooled and by Disclosure Cost) Notes. In panel A, the marginal effects in the “Pooled” column are based on the underlying regression inTable 10(“Payoff (Unconditional)”). The marginal effects by disclosure cost are from a similar regression, though with indicator variables for the actual disclosure cost incurred by the informed player. Payoffs for the informed player are inclusive of any disclosure cost incurred by the informed player. In panel A, the marginal effects are derived from analogous random effects regression, where the dependent variable in each regression is the payoff to the uninformed player. The last result (iii), that disclosure benefits informed typeHplayers and hurts informed typeLplayers, can be seen more clearly inTable 11, panel A, where we depict the marginal effect of disclosure on informed players’ payoffs. We do so both pooling over all disclosure costs and also broken down by disclosure cost. As one can observe, overall, disclosure is beneficial to the informed typeHplayer (first row, positive marginal effects) and harmful to the informed typeLplayer (second row, negative marginal effects). InTable 11, panel B, we report the same marginal effects for the uninformed player. In the table, disclosure is significantly positive for the typeHplayer (first row). Interestingly, whereas there should be no difference for the uninformed player depending on disclosure cost, we see that the effect is lowest (and only marginally significant) when the cost was $2.75, suggesting that informed players may have been able to partially recover some of the disclosure cost. In contrast, disclosure has a modest negative effect for typeLplayers (second row), though the effect is only significant when the disclosure cost is $0.75. This is not too surprising because disclosure increases the likelihood of being matched with a typeLinformed player, reducing the pie from $20 to $16. Furthermore, we have seen evidence that informed players who disclose recover some of the disclosure cost. These two negative effects cannot be offset by the increased likelihood of agreement that is also associated with disclosure. We summarize our findings as follows: (Disclosure for Matching).Consistent with the theoretical predictions, high informed types disclose significantly more often than low informed types, and disagreements are rare following disclosure. Disclosure declines as the cost of disclosure increases. Disclosure significantly benefits both informed and uninformed high types, and it significantly harms informed low types.
article.6. Robustness Experiments: We designed and parameterized our experiment in order to create a tension between the two roles for disclosure: (i) low types disclose to avoid disagreement, and (ii) high types disclose to secure a better match. Our results show directional support for both roles for disclosure. However, as can be seen inFigure 1, different parameterizations can lead to different disclosure equilibria when disclosure can influence matching. Therefore, it is also interesting to consider a different parameterization to see how disclosure behavior changes and whether it changes in a manner consistent with predictions. To this end, below we present a brief summary of results of additional experiments that utilized different experimental parameters and protocols. In a previous version of this paper, we reported results from two related experiments designed to address our research questions. To avoid confusion, we will refer to them here as “RE1” and “RE2.” The two experiments included 134 participants and followed similar protocols as in our current experiment. The first experiment, RE1, was designed to study the role of disclosure to avoid disagreement (coinciding with part 2 of our current experiment), whereas the second experiment, RE2, was designed to study the role of disclosure to influence matching (coinciding with part 3 of our current experiment). Importantly, both experiments had some design differences that allow us to speak to the generality and robustness of our results. We provide a brief summary of these past experiments and results here. The interested reader is referred to Online Appendix B for more details. In RE1, unlike our current experiment, we had a single uninformed type and four informed types corresponding to pie sizes of $12, $18, $24, and $30; we also had costless disclosure,d= 0. Theory predicts that, under the efficient equilibrium, only the lowest informed type strictly prefers to disclose, whereas the second lowest type ($18) is indifferent. For the equal-split norm, the three lowest types strictly preferred to disclose, and the highest type ($30) is indifferent. Our results generally supported the efficient equilibrium norm: disclosure rates were approximately 85%, 54%, 27%, and 11% for the informed types $12, $18, $24, and $30, respectively. We also saw that agreement rates were substantially higher when disclosure happened than when it did not, further validating a key role for disclosure under exogenous matching: avoiding disagreement. One interesting design feature from RE1, which we omitted from our current experiment, was the elicitation of beliefs by uninformed players about how likely each informed type was to disclose. In theory, an uninformed player should not make any inferences on the equilibrium path about their partner’s type following nondisclosure (other than that they are not the type(s) that are predicted to disclose). However, we observed that uninformed players make accurate inferences. Uninformed players correctly infer that the likelihood of disclosure is decreasing in the informed player’s type. Therefore, nondisclosure should lead to the uninformed player updating their beliefs and increasing the perceived likelihood of bargaining against a higher-informed player type. An interesting result that we found is that uninformed players who make stronger inferences appear to bargain more aggressively following nondisclosure and, consequently, earn higher payoffs. Managerially, these findings indicate that an uninformed player, such as a buyer negotiating with a supplier who has private information, should be able to infer what type of supplier they are negotiating with and make the appropriate adjustments during the bargaining process (e.g., bargain aggressively when the supplier does not reveal their private costs and vice versa). This result validates anecdotal evidence revealed to us by a manager. The manager indicated that the same suppliers who refuse to reveal their private costs are often very willing to reduce their actual prices significantly when asked to justify their pricing. This suggests that these firms are the “high” types who have the flexibility to lower their prices. RE2 was divided into two parts (without and with disclosure) and was virtually identical to parts 1 and 3 of our current experiment, with the main exception being that the parameters were different. Specifically, the parameters used were(𝜋ℎ,𝜋𝑙,𝛼ℎ,𝑑)=(24,16,1.5,0)(πh,πl,αh,d)=(24,16,1.5,0). Because of this, the predictions on which informed types should disclose were both different and somewhat less clear than in our current experiment (see Figure B.1 in the Online Appendix). For instance, for both bargaining norms, there were multiple disclosure equilibria at the chosen parameters. More importantly, unlike our current experiment, it was not an equilibrium for only the informed high type to disclose. Given this, depending on the equilibrium played, either low and high types should disclose with the same frequency or low types should actually disclose more than high types. Turning to the data, we do indeed see that the disclosure rates are virtually identical—approximately 40%—for the two informed types, consistent with the predictions. This suggests that players respond to incentives in a manner consistent with theory: high types disclose with greater frequency when it is in their interest to; otherwise, they do not.
article.7. Discussion: In this study, we find that voluntary disclosure of private information can have a meaningful impact on matching and bargaining outcomes. Theoretically, both the efficient and equal-split equilibria predict that when matching is not a concern, disclosure helps the relatively low informed player types, though the specific types helped depend on the bargaining norm. This is primarily because disclosure restores complete information and therefore eliminates the possibility of disagreement, which is more likely to occur when bargaining against the relatively low player types. Of course, when disagreement is not predicted (e.g., for the relatively high types in the efficient equilibrium and the highest type in the equal-split equilibrium), the payoff consequences of disclosure are neutral at best and harmful at worst. For example, under the efficient equilibrium, high types would sacrifice their information rents by disclosing. We find strong empirical support—part 2 of our experiment and RE1 of our robustness experiment—for our theoretical result that when voluntary disclosure is available after matching but before a negotiation, low informed player types can benefit from disclosure because of the reduced possibility of disagreement. In contrast, high informed types generally suffer from disclosure because the loss in information rents combined with the direct cost of disclosure outweighs any beneficial effect from a reduced likelihood of disagreement. From a managerial perspective, this result suggests that informed players with low types, such as suppliers with high private costs or retailers with poor predicted demand, should consider disclosing this information to partner companiesifthey are already engaged in a contract negotiation. In contrast, companies with more favorable private information, such as suppliers with lower costs or retailers with higher predicted demand, should prefer to keep that information confidential during any bargaining process. When we introduce a unique motivation for disclosure, which is often observed in practice—to influence the type of uninformed player one matches with—matters become more complicated theoretically because of the multiplicity of equilibria that is common in signaling games. Informed high player types may prefer to disclose if the increased match quality more than offsets the potential loss of information rents during bargaining. On the other hand, an informed low player type may also prefer to disclose because the increased chance of agreement may offset the lower match quality that comes from disclosure. From a theoretical perspective, whether any informed player type discloses depends on the underlying parameters and, also, the focal point equilibrium considered. Given this, and because many of these decisions are often made by human managers in practice, experimentation is a useful tool to provide managerial insights. To differentiate the two motivations for disclosure, in part 3 of our experiment, we deliberately chose parameters such that the unique disclosure equilibrium was foronlythe informed high type to disclose. However, the different parameterization from RE2 of our robustness experiment is also useful because it generates a different theoretical prediction—namely, that disclosure rates should be similar for both informed types. Thus, we can see whether disclosure patterns change as the theoretical predictions change. In sum, we find broad support for the theory. In part 3, where only high types should disclose, we find that they do disclose significantly more often than low types, and they generally benefit from disclosure, whereas low types generally suffer. Moreover, in RE2, where behavior is predicted to be similar across types, we do indeed see similar disclosure rates across the types. These results show that informed players who may feel inclined to reveal their private information need to consider several questions: how will it affect the likelihood of matching with a valuable counterpart, what information rents are being given up, and how will it affect the likelihood of reaching an agreement? Depending on one’s own type and these economic/strategic calculations, disclosure may or may not be beneficial. Note also that from an academic research perspective, existing papers on bargaining typically match two players together randomly in a negotiation. Yet our results indicate that the way in which two players are matched can have significant consequences on decisions and outcomes. For instance, when randomly matched, we observe that low informed player types benefit from disclosure, but when disclosure affects matching,15disclosure does not necessarily benefit the low informed player. Neglecting to capture both matching environments can lead to insights that may not be general or, worse, applied erroneously in practice. Another important beneficiary of disclosure is the uninformed high types. When disclosure occurs after matching, uninformed high types benefit from the reduced likelihood of disagreement and, if an informed high type erroneously discloses, through a larger share. When disclosure occurs before matching, they also benefit from being matched with a better-informed player type. Interestingly, we find evidence that informed players who disclosed were able to claw back some of the disclosure cost during bargaining, making uninformed players bear part of the cost of disclosure. Despite this cost, uninformed high types typically benefited from disclosure by informed players in both parts 2 and 3 (as well as in the robustness experiments reported inSection 6). This translates into another key managerial insight in that uninformed high player types, such as buyers with a large demand base, should seek to impose disclosure requirements on their partners as a condition of doing business. Whereas we believe our study advances the literature by examining how voluntary disclosure impacts matching and bargaining, we acknowledge that it is not without limitations. We considered a simple setting with few player types. We did this in an effort to examine a parsimonious setting that would establish a set of clear results. Future work could leverage this by investigating larger markets with more player types, although we admit that this may be challenging, both theoretically and experimentally. There could also be other factors, such as joint collaborative efforts after bargaining, which are not captured by our model but could change the profit calculus around disclosure. Another limitation is that we did not permit any type of communication. Allowing informed and uninformed players to communicate (or make promises in different stages of the game) and then letting them decide who to match with could be interesting. Overall, we believe that there are a number of exciting opportunities for future work in this area. We hope that our results can serve as a launching pad for such studies, which should translate into further managerial insights.
